headline: Bill Bunton Hanged by Montana Vigilantes, 1864
body:
The Montana wind bit deep, a raw, unforgiving January gale that whipped across the Ruby River. It was January 18, 1864, and the noose awaited Bill Bunton at his Cottonwood Ranch. The Vigilantes, a grim, self-appointed jury of the frontier, had come for him. Law was a distant whisper in these gold-crazed hills, and order, when it came, arrived with a rope and a cold, hard stare. Bunton, a man whose hands knew the feel of both cards and cold steel, was no stranger to the shadows that clung to the mining camps. He was a Road Agent, a member of Henry Plummer’s notorious gang, a wolf among wolves in a land where fortunes were made and lost in a single pan of gravel. 

His role was that of the stool pigeon, the inside man, the one who played the victim while orchestrating the plunder. Red, Erastus Yager, a fellow traveler on the dark road, had sung like a canary to the Vigilantes, naming Bunton as second in command. The details of the Peabody & Caldwell coach robbery, a tale of staged delays and feigned helplessness, had been laid bare. Bunton, with a theatrical flourish, had cried out during the ambush, “Oh! for God’s sake, men don’t kill one.” He had even tossed his own purse, a paltry hundred and twenty dollars, into the dust, a performance designed to cement his innocence in the eyes of his unwitting fellow passengers. But the Vigilantes saw through the charade. They had seen too many like him, men whose smiles hid daggers, whose pleas were but a prelude to betrayal. 

When they finally cornered him at his saloon, the scent of stale whiskey and fear hung heavy in the air. He refused to open the door, a cornered rat clinging to the last vestiges of his freedom. But the Vigilantes were relentless. They forced their way in, their faces hard, their purpose clear. A small Derringer, a pocket-sized harbinger of death, was later found in his vest, a gift from some unseen sympathizer, a final, futile gesture of defiance. He denied everything, the coach robbery, the butchering of Jack Thomas’ cattle—crimes that Red, his erstwhile accomplice, had confessed to being hired for by Bunton himself. But denials were thin gruel to men who had seen their comrades robbed, their gold stolen, and their dreams shattered by the likes of Bunton. The vote was taken, a silent, solemn raising of hands, and the verdict was unanimous: guilty. 

Before the drop, he passed his gold watch to his partner, Cooke, a final settlement of earthly accounts. His other possessions, the meager spoils of a life lived on the edge, were to pay his debts. He had won his share of the saloon through the turn of a card, a gambler to the last. The sun dipped below the jagged peaks, casting long, skeletal shadows across the Ruby River. The air grew colder, the wind keening a mournful tune. The Vigilantes, grim and unyielding, stood as silent witnesses to the harsh justice of the frontier. The rope tightened, a final, brutal embrace. The gold rush had brought men seeking fortune, but for some, it delivered only the cold, hard embrace of the gallows.
